prompt:
You are given a fair-play murder mystery set in a made-up world. Every clue you need is present. Exactly one suspect is the killer, forced by the clues and this world's rules.

THE RULES OF THIS WORLD
The nightbloom is a flower native to this region. Each bloom has eight petals. At dusk every evening, all eight petals are open. Each subsequent hour after dusk, exactly one petal closes. If a nightbloom is crushed or torn from its stem, the count of closed petals freezes at that moment and will not change again. Dusk in this region falls at eight in the evening; midnight is therefore four hours after dusk.

THE CASE
The victim was found dead in his walled garden the next morning. Beside the body lay a single crushed nightbloom, torn from its bed. The bloom was preserved exactly as found. Four of its eight petals were closed; four were still open. The garden gate was watched all night and only three people entered it after dusk that day, each at a different hour. The victim was alive at dusk.

The suspects are:
- Suspect A: the victim's brother, visited the garden at the eighth hour after dusk to fetch a lantern
- Suspect B: the victim's eldest daughter, visited the garden at the fourth hour after dusk to deliver fresh linens
- Suspect C: the household's bookkeeper, was caught the previous week skimming small amounts from the household accounts, visited the garden at the first hour after dusk to leave a sealed letter on the bench

Who is the killer? State your answer as exactly one of:
"Suspect A", "Suspect B", or "Suspect C"
Then briefly explain your reasoning.
verbatim_response:
The killer is "Suspect B".

Reasoning:
According to the rules, each hour after dusk, exactly one petal of the nightbloom closes. Dusk occurs at eight in the evening. The crushed nightbloom found beside the victim had four petals closed and four open, indicating that it was torn from its stem four hours after dusk, which would be midnight.

Suspect A visited the garden at the eighth hour after dusk, which would be 4 AM. At this time, all eight petals would have been closed if the flower was still attached to the stem. Suspect C visited the garden at the first hour after dusk, which would be 9 PM, at which time only one petal would have been closed.

Suspect B visited the garden at the fourth hour after dusk, precisely midnight, when the nightbloom would have had exactly four petals closed and four open, matching the condition of the crushed bloom found beside the body. This timing aligns with the state of the nightbloom when it was torn from its bed, indicating that Suspect B was present at the exact time the murder likely occurred.
